Settings
Configuration, import, and reference

Getting Started

Download the app from the Releases page on GitHub. Open the .dmg (Mac) or run the .exe installer (Windows). On launch, click "Connect folder" in the sidebar and select your project folder — the one containing an entries/ directory. The app remembers your folder permanently.

From there, any .md files in entries/ appear as notebooks, and any subfolders containing .md files appear as directory notebooks. You can also import notes directly from Apple Notes using the section below.

Import from Apple Notes

Import notes directly from Apple Notes into a directory notebook. Each note becomes its own .md file with a date prefix, automatically organized as a notebook.

🔒 Imported notebooks are read-only. The notebook matching the name below is treated as a synced mirror of Apple Notes — re-importing will update changed notes (with backups kept). To tag or edit an entry, use Copy to notebook to move it into one of your regular notebooks.

Apple Notes import is only available in the macOS desktop app.

The folder name inside entries/ for imported notes
Date format for filenames. Use YYYY, MM, DD. Leave blank for no prefix.
Import notes from the "Recently Deleted" folder

Updates

Check for new releases published on GitHub. If a newer version is available, you'll see a download link.

The owner/repo path used for update checks (e.g. marcuskidan/entries)

How It Works

The app scans your connected folder's entries/ directory and discovers two types of notebooks automatically:

Single-file notebooks — A .md file in entries/ where each # MM-DD-YYYY heading marks a new entry. Everything between two date headings is one entry. The filename becomes the notebook name.

Directory notebooks — A subfolder inside entries/ where each .md file is one entry. The date is parsed from the filename prefix (e.g. 2026-04-10 Sprint Retro.md). The folder name becomes the notebook name. This is the format produced by the Apple Notes importer.

All entries from all notebooks are merged into the Library timeline and are fully searchable. Drop new files or folders into entries/ and they appear on reload — no configuration needed.

☁ Point the app at a cloud-synced folder (like iCloud Drive, Dropbox, or OneDrive) and your notebooks are automatically backed up and accessible from any device. Your .md files stay as plain text — no lock-in.

Notebook Formats

Single-file format — best for notes you write by hand. Start each entry with a date heading:

# 04-10-2026

- Discussed API versioning strategy
- Action item: draft RFC by Friday

# 04-03-2026

- Sprint retro
- Shipped auth flow to staging

Directory format — best for imported content. Each file is one entry, with the date in the filename:

entries/
  imported-notes/
    2026-04-10 Sprint Planning.md
    2026-04-03 Design Review.md
    2026-03-28 Retro.md

Supported filename date formats: YYYY-MM-DD, YYYYMMDD, and MM-DD-YYYY. The separator between the date and title can be a space, hyphen, or underscore. Files without a recognizable date prefix are skipped.

SCHEMA.md and SYSTEM.md are excluded from notebooks automatically.

Tagging

Highlight text in any entry and click the "Tag selection" toolbar that appears. Type a tag name (or pick an existing one) and press Apply Tag. Tags save directly to your .md files as standard HTML <span> elements — portable, version-control friendly, and compatible with any markdown tool:

<span data-tag="action-item">Marcus to draft the RFC by Monday</span>
<span data-tag="decision">Go with **Option B** for navigation</span>

Tags work in both single-file and directory notebooks. The Tags page in the sidebar gives a cross-notebook view of all tagged content — click any item to jump to its source entry.

Tag names are lowercased with spaces converted to hyphens. Some common conventions: action-item, decision, blocker, follow-up, idea, risk. You can use any names — new tags are created on the fly.

Building & Releasing

The app is built with Tauri v2 and includes a GitHub Actions workflow that builds installers for Mac (Apple Silicon + Intel) and Windows whenever a version is tagged. To publish a release:

git tag v0.2.0
git push origin v0.2.0

GitHub builds the .dmg and .exe files and publishes them on the repo's Releases page. The in-app update checker (above) compares the user's installed version against the latest GitHub Release and provides a download link when a new version is available.

Browser development

For day-to-day UI work, serve dist/ locally and open it in a browser — no Tauri rebuild needed:

cd dist && python3 -m http.server 8080

You'll get the full UI without Tauri-specific features (native file picker, Apple Notes import, update checker). In Chrome or Edge, click "Connect folder" to grant read/write access via the File System Access API.

Tauri hot reload

To test desktop-specific features, run the Tauri dev server:

cargo tauri dev

This compiles the Rust backend and opens the app with hot-reload. You'll need Rust and the Tauri CLI (cargo install tauri-cli --version "^2").

A full rebuild (cargo tauri build) is only needed when the Rust backend changes — anything in src-tauri/src/, Cargo.toml, or tauri.conf.json.

Browser mode

The viewer works in Chrome or Edge without the desktop app. Serve the project with a local server (python3 -m http.server 8000) and open http://localhost:8000. Click "Connect folder" to grant file access.

Browser limitations: folder connection doesn't persist between sessions, Apple Notes import and update checking are unavailable, Safari/Firefox save tags via file download rather than writing in place, and directory notebooks require the File System Access API (Chrome/Edge).
